planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in fall or softwood cuttings in summer, or plant container-grown shrubs and trees in spring; crape myrtles are extremely drought-tolerant and heat-loving once established — they bloom best in the hottest, sunniest conditions. Avoid over-watering or over-fertilizing, which produces excessive foliage at the expense of flowers. Never 'crape murder' (severely top the plant) — prune only lightly to shape.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; crape myrtle is arguably the most important ornamental tree of the Gulf Coast, the mid-South, and California, producing dramatic clusters (panicles) of crepe-textured flowers in white, pink, red, lavender, or purple from July through September — among the longest bloom periods of any flowering tree — on plants that range from 3-foot dwarf shrubs to 30-foot trees depending on selection. The modern hybrid varieties (L. indica x L. fauriei) also offer spectacular exfoliating bark in cinnamon, gray, and russet that is ornamental year-round, plus brilliant orange-red fall color. These hybrids are also substantially more mildew-resistant than pure L. indica. Sizes range from true dwarf cultivars ('Petite Embers,' 'Tonto') to large trees ('Natchez,' 'Muskogee'). All are extremely heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant, and long-lived in zones 7–9. Spring pruning should be limited to removing dead wood and crossing branches — severe topping destroys the natural form permanently.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mastic tree requires excellent drainage and is killed by waterlogged soils. It tolerates drought, salt spray, rocky soils, and alkaline conditions that defeat most ornamental shrubs. It establishes slowly but becomes extremely drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; mastic tree is the source of the original mastic gum — the resinous, pine-scented exudate collected by scoring the bark of trees on the Greek island of Chios, used for at least 2,500 years as a chewing gum, food flavoring, incense, varnish, and medicine. Greek mastic ('Chios masticha') is a protected designation of origin product used in the liqueur Mastika, Greek chewing gum, breads, pastries, and Turkish delight. As a landscape plant, mastic tree is an outstanding drought-tolerant, evergreen shrub for Mediterranean-climate and Gulf Coast gardens, producing aromatic, glossy, pinnate leaves and small clusters of red to black berries that attract birds. It is excellent for coastal gardens due to its salt tolerance and thrives in the hot, alkaline, well-drained conditions of the Texas Hill Country and Gulf Coast. A relative of the pistachio tree (Pistacia vera).

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown vines after last frost in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; orchid vine is damaged below 30°F. In zone 9 it may die back in cold winters but resprouts vigorously from established roots. It grows rapidly in warm conditions and needs a strong trellis, fence, or support structure.

directions: Root cuttings in warm conditions and train on a trellis or fence in full sun to part shade; orchid vine (butterfly vine) is a South American twining vine producing clusters of intricate, butter-yellow flowers with fringed, wavy petals that closely resemble small orchid flowers or the wings of a butterfly, giving the plant both its common names. The flowers are followed by samara-style winged fruits that are ornamental in their own right. Stigmaphyllon ciliatum grows rapidly to 10–25 feet and provides attractive coverage for fences, trellises, pergolas, and arbors with its somewhat heart-shaped, textured leaves and near-constant bloom from summer through fall. The unusual flower form makes it immediately recognizable and a conversation piece in the garden. Related S. littorale (Amazon vine) is more salt-tolerant and suitable for coastal Gulf Coast conditions. An excellent and underused tropical vine for zone 9–11 gardens.

planting.note: Root cuttings or plant container-grown vines in spring; cat's claw vine climbs by hooked, three-pronged tendrils that grip almost any surface — it needs no additional support and can damage wood and masonry over time. Note: it is classified as invasive in many US states and several countries — confirm it is not invasive in your region before planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun to part shade; cat's claw vine is a vigorous semi-evergreen to evergreen climbing vine named for its distinctive three-pronged, hook-like tendrils that grip surfaces like a cat's claw, enabling it to climb rough masonry, wood, bark, and wire without additional support. In spring it produces a spectacular flush of large, bright-yellow trumpet-shaped flowers (2–3 inches) that cover the vine with color for several weeks. The foliage is attractive — glossy, dark-green, paired leaflets — and nearly evergreen in mild climates. It establishes quickly, grows vigorously, and provides dense coverage for walls, fences, and trellises with minimal maintenance. Caution: cat's claw vine is classified as invasive in Florida, parts of California, and other regions where it naturalizes aggressively. Always verify local regulations before planting and prevent it from spreading beyond its site.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or summer; unlike most Brugmansia, B. sanguinea is adapted to cool Andean conditions and actually prefers temperatures of 50–70°F for best bloom. In hot Gulf Coast summers it may decline — site in afternoon shade, keep well-watered, and expect the best display in spring and fall. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Root cuttings and grow in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in part shade; red angel's trumpet is the most distinctive Brugmansia species — unlike the white, pink, and yellow angels' trumpets (B. x candida and relatives) that prefer warm conditions, B. sanguinea is native to the cool, cloud forest elevations of the Andes (5,000–11,000 feet) in Peru, Chile, Colombia, and Ecuador, producing exceptionally large, pendant, bicolored tubular flowers that are red-orange at the flared tip fading to yellow at the base, with a prominent green calyx. The flowers are in continuous production on established plants and are pollinated by hummingbirds in their native habitat — making them strongly attractive to hummingbirds in cultivation. The cool-preference nature makes it better suited to coastal California and mild Pacific Northwest climates than to tropical Gulf Coast summers, where afternoon shade, ample water, and well-drained fertile soil are essential. All plant parts are highly toxic; contain scopolamine, atropine, and related tropane alkaloids.
